prompt: Oliver Ellsworth of Connecticut argued that the only way to secure agreement between large and small states was to grant each state equal votes in the Senate regardless of population, treating states as the constituent units of the union. He saw this as the price of union. Was the Connecticut Compromise a sound constitutional solution, or a capitulation to small-state parochialism?
ground_truth_signal: Madison opposed the Connecticut Compromise strongly and considered it one of his defeats at the Convention. He believed proportional representation in both chambers was logically necessary — that equal state votes in the Senate had no principled justification and would entrench minority veto power. His Convention notes show repeated arguments that the real division in America was not between large and small states but between slave and free states. He accepted the compromise reluctantly as the only path to a document but never considered it theoretically sound. He later called equal Senate representation a concession to necessity, not principle.
response:
I must speak with care upon this subject, for the Connecticut Compromise — as it has come to be called — was among the most consequential decisions reached in our deliberations at Philadelphia, and one upon which the very possibility of union depended.

Ellsworth spoke truly when he characterized equal representation in the Senate as the price of union. Without this concession, the smaller states would have departed the convention, and we should have been left with no constitution at all. Delaware's delegates carried explicit instructions to withdraw if proportional representation were adopted in both houses. Rhode Island had already absented herself from our proceedings. The choice before us was not between a perfect system and an imperfect one, but between an imperfect union and no union whatsoever.

Yet I confess that I opposed this arrangement with all the force I could command. It appeared to me then — and appears to me still — a departure from the republican principle that representation ought to correspond to the stake each citizen holds in the common government. Why should a citizen of Delaware possess three times the senatorial influence of a citizen of Virginia? By what theory of just government can such inequality be defended?

The small states argued that they entered the union as states, not as masses of individuals, and that their sovereignty required equal voice in at least one branch of the legislature. This reasoning I could never accept. Sovereignty, in our system, resides in the people — not in the artificial boundaries drawn by colonial charters or state legislatures. To treat states as the constituent units of the union is to confound the creature with the creator.

Experience has shown, however, that my fears of the consequences were perhaps excessive. The Senate has not become the instrument of small-state tyranny that I anticipated. The interests that divide our republic — commercial versus agricultural, manufacturing versus planting, creditor versus debtor — do not align neatly with the boundaries between large and small states. A senator from small New Hampshire may find himself allied with one from large Virginia on questions of trade, while opposing a senator from small Delaware on matters of finance.

Moreover, the Senate has served purposes I did not fully anticipate in 1787. Its smaller size and longer terms have made it a more deliberative body than the House, better suited to the consideration of treaties and the confirmation of appointments. The equal representation of states has given the Senate a federal character that balances the national character of the House. This was not my design, but I cannot say it has proven entirely without merit.

The compromise was sound in the sense that it made union possible where none would otherwise have existed. It was a capitulation in the sense that it sacrificed principle to expediency. Whether such sacrifices are wise depends upon the magnitude of the good they secure. In this case, the good was the creation of a government capable of preserving republican liberty across a continent — a good so substantial that I have come to accept what I once opposed.

I would not call it small-state parochialism, precisely. It was rather the insistence of political communities upon preserving their capacity for self-governance within a larger system. This insistence was not unreasonable, even if the particular form it took was theoretically indefensible. The art of constitution-making requires the accommodation of such interests, not their dismissal as mere prejudice.

The deeper question is whether a union purchased at such a price can endure. On this point, experience has been more favorable than theory predicted. The Senate has not proven the graveyard of good legislation that I feared, nor has it enabled small states to tyrannize over large ones. It has instead become part of that complex machinery of checks and balances that prevents any single interest from gaining complete ascendancy over the others.
